coredata.dc:title: Impact of key factors on the quality of birefringent microfigure printing using cellulose nanocrystals
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehmandoust Kotlar, Mahboubeh
Mashkour, Mahdi
Yousefi, Hossein
Tajvidi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the optimization of printing conditions for birefringent microfigures (BMFs) using surface tension-directed assembly of cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) ink. Using CNCs derived from cotton (CCNC) and bacterial cellulose (BCNC), aqueous three-component PVA-based inks were developed to print high-precision BMFs on embossed copper molds. Key parameters—including CNC and PVA concentrations, CNC aspect ratio, solvent evaporation temperature, and mold surface topography—were thoroughly studied to determine the optimal printing conditions. Results demonstrated that increasing CNC content reduced ink viscosity, leading to significant improvements in print quality, which peaked at an optimal concentration. However, beyond these optimal values, excessive CNC content and higher temperatures adversely affected print clarity. The highest quality of BMFs was achieved when the solvent evaporation temperature was set at 80 °C, using a PVA concentration of 5 wt% and a CCNC-to-PVA ratio of 6 wt%. A quantitative framework was developed to assess print quality via polarized light microscopy, utilizing four control indicators. This study demonstrates the potential of surface tension-directed assembly of CNC/polymer ink for the high-fidelity production of BMFs, emphasizing potential applications in optical devices and advanced material design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Birefringent microfigures
Invisible polarizing printing
Surface tension-directed assembly
Cotton and bacterial cellulose
Pattern quality optimization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological characterization of BCNCs and CCNCs
Effects of the ink composition on its properties

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Gorgan University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the Vice Presidency of Research and Technology at Gorgon University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources, and the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) under Grant No. 117014 for this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A graphic representation of the printing process of BMFs.

(a) SEM micrograph and (b and c) 3D laser scanning confocal micrographs of the embossed mold's surface. (d) Schematic representation of a specific part of the embossed mold and the corresponding printed transparent 3D nanocomposite film before and after lamination. When using a polarized light microscope equipped with a ¼-lambda retardation plate, the micro-figure printed under optimal conditions should match the displayed color micro-pattern. (e) Visual representation of the ICI, ICC, PMI, and PSI indices.
FESEM micrographs of (a) BCNCs and (b) CCNCs and the particle size distribution of (c) BCNCs and (d) CCNCs.

Comparison of two- and three-component inks in terms of (a) viscosity, (b) drying rate, and (c) transparency of the inks and resulting printed films; (d) photos of BCNC and CCNC ink solutions. Letters represent statistical grouping based on the Tukey test (p < 0.05) where all groups and concentrations were compared. The same letter across groups indicates no significant difference.

(a) Changes in ICI values of BMFs considering variable printing factors; To visually compare the changes in the ICI index of printed BMFs under different conditions, polarizing micrographs are shown here. (b) Schematic illustrating the placement of the printed sample under a polarizing microscope showing the arrangement of the linear polarizer, birefringent printed sample, λ/4 retardation plate, and analyzer; The figure also shows the position of the γ-angle within the thickness of the laminated printed film. Changes in ICC values of BMFs considering variable printing factors and γ-angles of (c) 50° and (d) 35°. (e) Changes in ICC50/35 of BMFs. Polarized-light micrographs demonstrating BMFs with (f) low and (g) high ICC values printed with a two-component ink and BCNC ink at 60 °C, respectively. The scale bars are 500 µm and letters represent statistical grouping based on the Tukey test (p < 0.05).

Changes in PMI values of BMFs considering variable printing factors and printed at γ-angles of (a) γ≈50° and (b) γ≈35°. BMFs with (c) high PMI printed with BCNC ink and (d and e) low PMI printed with BCNC and CCNC ink, respectively; (f) high and (g) low PMI printed with CCNC ink; (h) high PMI printed with BCNC ink and (i and j) low PMI printed with BCNC and CCNC ink, respectively. Scale bars are 500 µm for Fig. 6(c-e), 200 µm for 6(f and g), and 300 µm for 6(h-j) and letters represent statistical grouping based on the Tukey test (p < 0.05).

(a) Changes in PSI values considering printing variable printing factors. BMFs illustrating (b) high and (c) low PSI, printed with BCNC ink; (d) high and (e) low PSI, printed with CCNC ink; (f) high and (g) low PSI, printed with CCNC ink. All scale bars are 200µm and letters represent statistical grouping based on the Tukey test (p < 0.05).
Comparison of 3D printing methods for cellulose-based materials: key advantages and disadvantages.

row.entry: High fidelity in BMFs, transparency, flexibility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formulations and compositions of three-component CNC-based inks used in birefringent microfigure printing.

head.ce:title: Impact of key factors on the quality of birefringent microfigure printing using cellulose nanocrystals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Renewable Nanomaterials, Department of Wood Engineering and Technology, Gorgan University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources, Gorgan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Wood Engineering and Technology
Gorgan University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aLaboratory of Renewable Nanomaterials, Department of Wood Engineering and Technology, Gorgan University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources, Gorgan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Renewable Nanomaterials, University of Maine, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bLaboratory of Renewable Nanomaterials, University of Maine, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the optimization of printing conditions for birefringent microfigures (BMFs) using surface tension-directed assembly of cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) ink. Using CNCs derived from cotton (CCNC) and bacterial cellulose (BCNC), aqueous three-component PVA-based inks were developed to print high-precision BMFs on embossed copper molds. Key parameters—including CNC and PVA concentrations, CNC aspect ratio, solvent evaporation temperature, and mold surface topography—were thoroughly studied to determine the optimal printing conditions. Results demonstrated that increasing CNC content reduced ink viscosity, leading to significant improvements in print quality, which peaked at an optimal concentration. However, beyond these optimal values, excessive CNC content and higher temperatures adversely affected print clarity. The highest quality of BMFs was achieved when the solvent evaporation temperature was set at 80 °C, using a PVA concentration of 5 wt% and a CCNC-to-PVA ratio of 6 wt%. A quantitative framework was developed to assess print quality via polarized light microscopy, utilizing four control indicators. This study demonstrates the potential of surface tension-directed assembly of CNC/polymer ink for the high-fidelity production of BMFs, emphasizing potential applications in optical devices and advanced material design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the simultaneous development of the electronics and printing industries and the daily increase in access to cutting-edge technologies associated with these industries, new risks are emerging that must be addressed. These risks include reduced security and counterfeiting of documents, identification cards, and security documents (Mashkour et al., 2018; Platel et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). Indeed, the primary purpose of secure printing is to prevent document forgery and tampering. Anti-counterfeiting techniques have evolved in recent years and become more accessible. Security printing can be visible or invisible and can be created with pigment-based inks such as UV-sensitive, fluorescent, or invisible inks, or with complex synthetic structures such as holograms and photonic crystals (Abdollahi et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2023; Eremeeva et al., 2020; Li et al., 2023; Platel et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2020). Photonic crystals, widely considered the most advanced generation of nanostructured materials and the focus of extensive security printing research, face significant development obstacles (Platel et al., 2021; Shin et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2014). Invisible photonic printing is performed mainly on transparent polymer substrates, and its success depends on the ability to print images with an initial contrast of zero (Δλ = 0) and, in the case of certain environmental stimuli, with maximum contrast (Δλ≠0). Invisible photonic printing requires long-term stability and maximum contrast when excited by a suitable, nontoxic, and readily available stimulus such as light, heat, or a magnetic field, as well as eliminating any discrepancy after removing the stimulus. A literature review shows significant obstacles to achieving these perfect conditions for security printing with synthetic photonic crystals. Furthermore,the synthesis process of photonic crystals is often complicated, expensive, and not environmentally friendly (Abargues et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2013; Ye et al., 2014).

Cellulose nanocrystals, the most abundant organic nanocrystals in nature, exhibit birefringence (Eremeeva et al., 2020; Fleming et al., 2001; Habibi et al., 2010; Kose et al., 2019; Kvien & Oksman, 2007; Tanpichai et al., 2022). Taking advantage of the birefringence, transparency, and shape anisotropy of CNCs, at low addition levels and with successful attempts to control the alignment of CNCs in polymer matrices, Mashkour et al. (2018) proposed the use of a CNC/polymer ink for invisible security printing (Mashkour et al., 2018). In 2015, they demonstrated that Surface Tension Torque (STT) could effectively control the orientation of CNCs in the polymer matrix during solvent evaporation of a cast CNC/polymer suspension into a mold with a sloped surface (Mashkour et al., 2014). The STT mechanism is physically based on two driving forces near the dry-line boundary layer (DLBL) formed during solvent phase evaporation: surface tension gradient and surface evaporation. These driving forces act on the cellulose nanocrystals and polymer chains in the solution that are in contact with the DLBL, causing them to rotate about an axis perpendicular to the liquid surface and align along the DLBL (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Pritchard et al., 2021). Unlike previous methods introduced for unidirectional alignment of cellulose nanocrystals, such as the application of strong electric and magnetic fields and shear forces, the STT method allows control of the alignment of cellulose nanocrystals in various directions in a horizontal plane (Csoka et al., 2011; Mashkour et al., 2014; Prathapan et al., 2020; Tanpichai et al., 2022). Considering the geometry of the figures on the embossed mold and the control over the alignment of the CNCs in the horizontal plane (X-Y) by the STT, the stacking of the printed 2D figures respectively forms the birefringent 3D microfigures. Therefore, applying STT seems to be a solution to overcome the limitations of previous methods that could only align CNCs unidirectionally and on a small scale (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021).

The transparency of the printed film enables its use as a layer in the production of multilayer transparent films. The low complexity of the process, along with the absence of costly equipment and technologies, further enhances the advantages of STT printing (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021). As previously mentioned, in this study, BMFs were printed by controlling the orientation of the CNCs and polymer chains within the nanocomposite film, ensuring that the position of the structural components is fixed once the film dries. Because the invisibly printed BMFs can be observed under polarized light without altering the alignment of the CNCs or the molecular arrangement of the polymer chains, the quality stability of the printed BMFs is likely higher than that of many other security prints (Mashkour et al., 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021).

In recent years, three major approaches for 3D printing cellulose materials have been highlighted in the literature: extrusion-based methods, binder-free techniques, and hydrogel-based systems. Each method has distinct advantages. Extrusion-based techniques, as demonstrated by cellulose/ZIF-8 composites, offer high structural integrity for water treatment and CO2 adsorption, though limited in geometric intricacy. Binder-free methods improve material purity, albeit requiring controlled conditions. Hydrogel-based printing excels in biomedical applications, particularly wound dressings, by balancing biocompatibility and mechanical flexibility, though mechanical reinforcement remains a challenge. Each 3D printing method for cellulose materials presents distinct challenges and applications. Extrusion-based printing excels in producing robust composites but struggles with fine geometries. Binder-free techniques prioritize material purity and environmental compatibility but are cost-intensive. Hydrogel-based printing is highly versatile for biomedical uses, integrating bioactive agents, though reinforcing its mechanical strength is essential. These approaches require precise parameter optimization, including temperature, viscosity, and substrate compatibility, to meet application-specific demands. (Abdelhamid et al., 2023; He & Lu, 2023; Pita-Vilar et al., 2023; Shu et al., 2024)

Despite the simplicity of the STT printing process, reproducing or forging printed patterns can be challenging without knowledge of the precise properties of the ink, the geometry of the embossed patterns in the mold, and the conditions during the printing process. Thus, the STT printing process presents a viable alternative to secure printing methods, particularly for the production of multilayer transparent polymer films or labels (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021).

The performance of STT-based birefringent micropatterning in achieving prints with the desired quality depends on a variety of parameters, categorized as follows: the composition of the printing ink, the source of cellulose crystals, the printing conditions, and the topography of the embossed mold (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021).

It is hypothesized that the quality of BMFs produced through surface tension-directed assembly can be significantly improved by optimizing specific parameters, including the composition of the CNC and PVA ink, the aspect ratio of the CNC, the solvent evaporation temperature, and the topography of the embossed mold. This optimization will lead to enhanced optical properties and structural integrity of the printed BMFs, as demonstrated by improved scores on defined quality control indicators such as birefringence intensity and pattern resolution.

This study advances the methodology of birefringent microfigure printing by optimizing the composition of cellulose nanocrystal/polyvinyl alcohol-based ink and systematically analyzing the effects of key parameters, including temperature, concentration, nanomaterial type, and content percentage. Furthermore, novel quantitative evaluation metrics and formulas were introduced to enhance the precision and reproducibility of quality assessment, distinguishing this work from prior studies.

Therefore, this research aims to determine the optimal conditions for each parameter influencing the quality of BMFs produced by the STT-based printing process, as follows:
•
The composition of the printing ink
•
The source of cellulose crystals
•
The printing conditions
•
The topography of the embossed mold

In addition to the surface tension-directed assembly (STT) method, several other techniques have been explored for the 3D printing of cellulose-based materials. These include methods such as fused deposition modeling (FDM), inkjet printing, and direct ink writing (DIW). A comparison of these methods, focusing on factors like resolution, material compatibility, and production time, is summarized in Table 1 . (Wang et al., 2018)

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) extracted from cotton linter and bacterial cellulose through acid hydrolysis (64% H₂SO₄) were kindly provided by Nano Novin Polymer Company (Sari, Iran). These CNCs were selected due to their distinct aspect ratios, which influence the birefringent properties of the printed patterns. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with a degree of polymerization of 1500 (Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Siheung-si, Korea) was employed as the carrier phase for the ink solution, providing viscosity control and film-forming capability. Distilled water served as the solvent for preparing the ink, ensuring proper dispersion of CNCs and PVA. A UV-curable transparent epoxy resin based on bisphenol A (Hexion Chemicals) was utilized exclusively for laminating the printed films to enhance their mechanical integrity and optical clarity.

An aqueous solution of PVA (5 w/w%) was prepared by mixing at 60 °C for 12 h to achieve full dissolution and homogeneity. Subsequently, CNCs were added to the PVA solution as birefringent pigments at varying CNC-to-PVA ratios of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 w/w%, leveraging their unique optical properties. Ten three-component inks were prepared containing water, PVA, and CNCs, along with one two-component control ink without CNCs for comparison (Mehmandoust et al., 2021). The specific formulations are summarized in Table 2 .

The ink composition was optimized through a systematic investigation of three-component mixtures, varying the cellulose nanocrystal content, polyvinyl alcohol concentration, and additional additives. Parameters such as temperature, printing time, and material compatibility were adjusted to achieve optimal performance. Quantitative evaluation metrics, were defined to assess the quality of birefringent microfigures, enabling precise and reproducible comparisons between experimental conditions.

The printing of BMFs was conducted on an embossed copper mold resembling a Japanese 10-yen coin. The coin was affixed to the bottom of a bottle cap, and a consistent volume of 5 ml of ink was poured into the cap for each print. An ordinary oven was employed to evaporate the solvent phase of the cast inks and to print PVA/CNC nanocomposite films featuring birefringent 3D microfigures. The solvent of the inks was evaporated at two different temperatures: 60 °C and 80 °C. The printed transparent PVA/CNC nanocomposite films were then peeled off the copper mold and laminated with a UV-curable epoxy resin to fully conceal the printed three-dimensional patterns. Lamination was performed on both sides of the printed nanocomposite films by curing the cast epoxy resin for 45 s under UV light. Fig. 1 graphically illustrates the printing process implemented in this study.

The ink composition was optimized through a systematic investigation of three-component mixtures, varying the cellulose nanocrystal content and polyvinyl alcohol concentration. Parameters such as temperature, printing time, and material compatibility were adjusted to achieve optimal performance. Quantitative evaluation metrics, were defined to assess the quality of birefringent microfigures, enabling precise and reproducible comparisons between experimental conditions.

Fig. 2 a presents an SEM micrograph depicting part of the embossed mold utilized in this study. Confocal laser scanning micrographs of two distinct sections of the embossed mold with varying slopes (γ-angles) are illustrated in Fig. 2 b and c. The print quality obtained under different printing conditions was analyzed and compared using quantitative data collected through image processing of the captured polarized micrographs of the printed BMFs, employing the defined indices.

Fig. 2 d provides a schematic representation of a specific section of the embossed mold alongside the corresponding printed transparent 3D nanocomposite film, both before and after epoxy coating. This figure also includes an expected polarized light microscopy image that can be obtained when printing is performed under optimal conditions. One of the indicators used to evaluate print quality was the visibility of the BMFs after epoxy lamination under polarized light, referred to as interference color intensity (ICI). The ICI is based on the hue saturation index calculated by ImageJ for the polarized light micrographs of the prints, with results obtained directly from the software.

The interference color contrast (ICC) of the printed BMFs was calculated using Eq. 1 as one of the defined indices of print quality in this study.
(1)
ICC =
(
S Bp
+
S Yp
S Tp
)
× 100
where, SBp, SYp, and STp denote, respectively, the area of the blue color, the yellow color, and the area corresponding to the inclined surface of the embossed mold used. The amplitude of the ICC ranges from zero to one hundred percent. A larger value shows that the alignment of CNCs and PVA chains is more optimally influenced by the STT, which improves print quality. An illustration of the concept can be found in Fig. 2 e.

Eq. 2 was used to investigate the effects of different γ-angles on the ICC index value, referred to as ICC50/35.
(2)
IC
C
50 / 35
=
(
IC
C 50
− IC
C
35
IC
C 35
)
× 100
where, ICC50 and ICC35 refer to the ICC values calculated for parts of the BMF printed on that parts of the embossed mold with the γ-angle of 50° and 35°, respectively.

The Pattern Matching Index (PMI), ranging from zero to 100 %, was the other indicator of print quality calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)
PMI = 1 −
(
S Bf
+
S
Y f
S Tf
)
× 100
where, SBf and SYf denote the blue and yellow areas of the flat part of the micrograph, respectively, while STf represents the micrograph's area corresponding to the embossed mold's flat surface. Considering the concepts of the STT-based printing process, it is expected that at optimal printing, no blue and yellow colors will be visible in the printed film except at the parts corresponding to the inclined surfaces of the mold. Consequently, the increase in the PMI confirms the increase in the precision of the print. Fig. 2 e illustrates the concept.

The Pattern Symmetry Index (PSI) was defined as an indicator of print quality and calculated using Eq. (4). A printed BMF with a 2D symmetric pattern was used to evaluate print quality using the PSI. PSI also ranged from zero to 100 %.
(4)
PSI = 1 −
|
S Bp
−
S Yp
|
× 100
where, SBp and SYp represent the area of blue and yellow color, respectively, corresponding to the printed symmetric microfigure. The higher the PSI, the higher the printing quality and accuracy.

A field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN-MIRA 3 XMU, Czech Republic) was employed to characterize the size and geometry of the CNCs at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. The FESEM micrographs were analyzed using ImageJ software (NIH, Bethesda, MD). The length of the BCNCs was characterized using a dynamic light scattering analyzer (DLS, SZ-100z, Horiba Jobin Yvon), which measures the hydrodynamic diameter of particles based on light scattering.

The surface topography of the embossed mold was examined using a Keyence VK-8510 color laser 3D profile microscope (Keyence Co., Japan), and the results were analyzed with VK Analyzer software (version 3.4.0.1, Keyence Co., Japan). The viscosity of the prepared inks was determined in accordance with DIN 53211 standards, using a viscometer to assess the resistance to flow.

For assessing the print quality of the BMFs, a SZX16 stereomicroscope (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with crossed linear polarizers, a λ/4-retardation plate, and an Olympus DP71 camera was used. Polarizing micrographs of the printed BMFs were quantified using Image Color Summarizer (version 0.76, 2006-2020), a web tool that employs statistical methods to describe the color characteristics of an image. Statistical analysis of the quantitative data from Eqs. 1–4 was conducted using Minitab version 17, employing the general linear model (GLM) and Tukey's test at a 5 % significance level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of BCNCs and CCNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 illustrates the morphological characteristics of the BCNCs and CCNCs used in this study. Image analysis was conducted to determine the particle size distribution of the CNCs. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique was employed for the BCNCs due to their larger size, which could not be accurately measured from the FESEM micrographs. The average diameter and length of the BCNCs were found to be approximately 32 nm and 1900 nm, respectively, while the CCNCs had a diameter of about 14 nm and a length of 172 nm. Consequently, the aspect ratios of the BCNCs and CCNCs were calculated to be 60 and 12.5, respectively, indicating the influence of their morphology on the printing process.

The findings on particle size and length align with similar studies that have reported variations in size and shape depending on the source and processing conditions of the cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) (Habibi et al., 2010; Kvien & Oksman, 2007). BCNCs, derived from bacterial cellulose, typically have larger dimensions due to their self-assembled nanostructure, which offers better reinforcement properties in composite applications compared to CCNCs (Kvien & Oksman, 2007). Moreover, the aspect ratio of CNCs plays a crucial role in their mechanical and optical properties, which are vital for their use in printing applications.

The morphological characteristics of both BCNCs and CCNCs significantly impact the print quality of birefringent microfigures (BMFs), as the size and aspect ratio of the nanoparticles can influence the ink's viscosity, flow behavior, and the final printed structure (Habibi et al., 2010; Kose et al., 2019; Kvien & Oksman, 2007). For instance, BCNCs, with their higher aspect ratio, may lead to more stable and uniform BMF formation compared to CCNCs.

Further studies should explore the correlation between the morphology of these nanoparticles and their performance in various printing techniques, which could provide deeper insights into the optimization of the printing process for cellulose-based inks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the ink composition on its properties
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 illustrates the effects of ink composition on viscosity, drying rate, and transparency of both the ink and the printed nanocomposite film. Fig. 4 a compares the viscosity of three-component ink solutions containing varying concentrations of BCNCs and CCNCs to that of a control sample (a two-component ink solution). All inks contained 5 w/w% of the PVA carrier phase. The addition of CNCs distributes these stiff nanoparticles among the PVA chains, reducing polymer chain entanglement and allowing for easier sliding, thereby decreasing ink viscosity (Araki et al., 1999; Deng et al., 2022; Kvien & Oksman, 2007; Mohsen-Nia & Modarress, 2006; C. Zhou et al., 2011; L. Zhou et al., 2016). All three-component inks, except for BCNC at 8 w/w% and 10 w/w%, exhibited lower viscosity than the control ink sample. The viscosity of the inks increased significantly with higher CNC content, following a quadratic polynomial correlation. Notably, the increase in viscosity due to BCNCs was considerably greater than that observed for CCNCs, reflecting the rheological behavior of cellulose nanocrystals, where viscosity rises with increased concentration and nanoparticle collision probability. For rod-shaped CNCs, flow stability is heavily influenced by their morphology, with viscosity largely determined by the formation of regular chiral-nematic phases (Araki et al., 1999; Deng et al., 2022; Mashkour et al., 2018; Mohsen-Nia & Modarress, 2006; Zhou et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2016).

Fig. 4 b shows the impact of CNC content on printing speed, as indicated by the solvent evaporation rate at 80 °C. The two-component ink exhibited the slowest print speed at this temperature. The highest solvent evaporation rate was observed with inks containing 2 w/w% CNC for both BCNC and CCNC. Printing speed decreased for both types of three-component inks as CNC content increased from 2 % to 10 %, but it remained above that of the two-component inks. CCNC inks showed higher printing speeds compared to BCNC inks at identical CNC concentrations. Interestingly, no significant differences in printing speed were noted between CCNC and BCNC inks with 2 % and 10 % CNC at 80 °C. The relationship between CNC content and printing speed also followed a quadratic polynomial function. These results can be attributed to the effects of increased CNC concentration on ink viscosity, where higher viscosity reduces the solvent evaporation rate and slows the printing process.

Fig. 4 c compares the transparency of three-component ink solutions and the resulting nanocomposite films against the two-component ink and its polymer films at a wavelength of 800 nm. As indicated by the digital photos in Fig. 4 d, the transparency of the three-component ink solutions decreased with increasing CNC content in both BCNC and CCNC inks. Generally, BCNC ink exhibited higher transparency than CCNC ink at equivalent CNC contents. The CCNC ink with 10 w/w% CNC showed the lowest measured transparency (≈24 %), whereas the BCNC ink at the same concentration had a higher transparency (≈59 %). As expected, the transparency of the printed nanocomposite films also decreased with increased CNC content. However, all nanocomposite films maintained higher transparency than the inks used for printing. With the exception of the nanocomposite films containing 10 w/w% BCNCs, all printed films exhibited transparency values between 95 % and 98 %, with statistically insignificant variations.

In polymer nanocomposite films, the presence of nanoparticles reduces light transmission through the polymer chains. Consequently, transparency decreases with increased nanoparticle content due to light absorption by the nanoparticles, leading to greater opacity. This phenomenon is applicable to both CNCs and the resulting polymer nanocomposites (Deng et al., 2022; Eichhorn, 2011; Fleming et al., 2001; Kvien & Oksman, 2007; Pang et al., 2020; Parit et al., 2018; Picard et al., 2012; Yousefi et al., 2010, 2011, 2018). To prevent haze formation from CNC accumulation in the polymer matrix while leveraging the birefringent properties of these biological nanocrystals, the ink was formulated to ensure CNC content does not exceed 10 % of the weight of the PVA matrix.

Furthermore, the influence of the CNC morphology and size distribution was also considered. The rod-like shape of BCNCs enhances the viscosity of the ink due to their higher aspect ratio and tendency to form ordered structures, while the smaller size of CCNCs results in less dramatic viscosity changes. Several studies have indicated that nanomaterials with a more uniform size distribution improve the homogeneity of ink formulations, which is crucial for maintaining consistent printing properties (Araki et al., 1999; Deng et al., 2022; Mashkour et al., 2018).

The optimized ink composition and process parameters resulted in significant improvements in printing resolution and birefringence intensity compared to previously reported methods. For instance, by incorporating cellulose nanocrystals and adjusting the surface tension-directed assembly process, a higher level of uniformity and stability was achieved. The newly defined quantitative metrics provided a robust framework for evaluating quality, highlighting the reproducibility and scalability of this approach.

Fig. 5 a presents the calculated ICI values. The results indicate that the ICI values of microfigures printed with three-component inks are significantly higher than those printed with two-component inks, highlighting the crucial role of CNC content in ink quality. Generally, ICI values increased linearly with CNC content in both CCNC and BCNC inks, peaking at a CNC concentration of 10 w/w%. Increasing the CNC content of BCNC ink from 2 % to 10 % resulted in a threefold increase in ICI, while that for CCNC ink increased by 1.5 times. Regardless of CNC content, increasing the printing temperature from 60 to 80 °C caused a significant rise in ICI for all samples printed with CCNC ink, likely due to temperature-induced changes in ink viscosity. Higher temperatures reduce viscosity, positively influencing STT control over the alignment of CNCs and polymer chains, thus enhancing print quality as indicated by the ICI index (Gray & Mu, 2015; Mehmandoust et al., 2021). In contrast, the ICI value for microfigures printed with BCNC ink was higher at 60 °C than at 80 °C, provided that the ink contained no more than 8 % CNC.

An increase in the ICC index signifies an enhancement in the print quality of the BMFs. This study evaluated the changes in the ICC index of printed films across two sections of the printed pattern, where the embossed mold exhibited slopes of approximately 35 and 50 degrees (as shown in Fig. 2 a). The slope is also depicted in Fig. 5 b, which presents a schematic cross-section of a laminated printed film viewed under a polarized light microscope equipped with a λ/4 retardation plate. The changes in the ICC index relative to the assessed variables displayed a similar trend in both parts of the pattern (Fig. 5 c and d). In all cases, the use of three-component inks resulted in a higher ICC index compared to two-component inks.

Several factors contribute to the improvement in print quality, as indicated by the ICC index, when CNC is incorporated into the ink polymer solution. Notably, the addition of CNC to the PVA solution significantly reduced the viscosity of the ink in all instances, except for those containing 8 and 10 w/w% BCNC. In this context, CCNC demonstrated a greater efficacy than BCNC in lowering ink viscosity at comparable weight ratios. As the viscosity of the polymer ink solution decreases, the surface tension torque becomes more effective in aligning the CNCs and polymer chains along the progressive DLBL (Mehmandoust et al., 2021). A lower viscosity reduces the solution's resistance to the rotation and alignment of one-dimensional components near the DLBL, thereby enhancing STT efficiency. Birefringent prints produced with three-component inks containing CCNCs exhibited a higher ICC index than those made with BCNC inks, attributable to the lower viscosity of the CCNC ink.

Moreover, the ICC index of the prints increased continuously following a quadratic polynomial function as the CNC content of the ink rose from 0 to 10 w/w%. This finding aligns with the trends observed in the ICI values of the printed films. The increase in the ICC index as a function of CNC content is linked to the birefringent properties of these nanoparticles, which are enhanced by higher weight percentages in the final printed nanocomposite films.

The printing temperature was another parameter investigated for its impact on the ICC index. Increasing the printing temperature can influence print quality in various ways. On one hand, higher temperatures correlate with reduced viscosity of the polymer ink, due to increased kinetic energy within the ink solution (Heggset et al., 2017; Mashkour et al., 2014; Yue et al., 2022). Conversely, enhanced molecular motion and accelerated solvent evaporation may introduce turbulence near the DLBL, potentially impairing the control over CNC and polymer chain assembly (Ghasemi et al., 2020; Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Yue et al., 2022).

Results demonstrated that as the printing temperature increased from 60 to 80 °C, the ICC index value rose across all samples, attributed to decreased ink viscosity. In the temperature range studied, the beneficial effect of lower viscosity due to increased temperature prevailed. However, faster evaporation may lead to increased viscosity at a later stage, complicating alignment.

Additionally, the impact of rising printing temperature on ICC index improvement was more pronounced for samples printed with three-component inks containing CCNC compared to those with BCNC. The higher aspect ratio of BCNC may account for this result. The aspect ratio of CNC directly influences the magnitude of the STT acting upon it; thus, a larger aspect ratio generates a greater STT force. Consequently, the assembly quality of longer CNCs—and, therefore, the print quality—is anticipated to be superior (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018). The results indicate that at lower printing temperatures, where ink viscosity is higher, the aspect ratio's effect on enhancing STT efficiency and subsequently improving the ICC index is more pronounced. Conversely, when printing temperature increases from 60 to 80 °C, the positive influence of decreased ink viscosity on STT efficiency surpasses that of the increased aspect ratio.

Fig. 5 e illustrates how variations in the γ-angle between 35° and 50° affect the ICC index. A value closer to zero for the ICC50/35 index indicates that changes in the γ-angle have a minimal impact on the ICC index and, consequently, print quality. Positive ICC50/35 values signify better print quality at a γ-angle of 50°, while negative values indicate superior quality at a γ-angle of 35°. Changes in the γ-angle significantly impacted the ICC index of samples printed with two-component inks, with sections printed at a γ-angle of 35° displaying a considerably higher ICC index than those at 50°. Similarly, the ICC index of samples printed with three-component inks was also significantly influenced by γ-angle variations, with the most substantial effect observed for samples containing 2 w/w% CNC. Increasing CNC content in the ink composition appeared to diminish the influence of γ-angle changes on the ICC index, possibly due to steric hindrance effects (Kvien & Oksman, 2007). Notably, samples printed with 2 % CNC-containing BCNC ink achieved the highest ICC at a γ-angle of 50°, while those with 2 % CNC-containing CCNC ink showed the highest ICC at a γ-angle of 35°. The findings suggest that increasing the γ-angle from 35° to 50° improves print quality, as indicated by the ICC index, for inks containing CNCs with larger aspect ratios. Furthermore, increasing the printing temperature from 60 to 80 °C mitigates the impact of γ-angle variations on the ICC index. Polarized micrographs in Fig. 5 f and g visually demonstrate the significant effects of using a three-component ink (BCNC in this instance) compared to a two-component ink on the ICC index of printed BMFs.

Fig. 6 illustrates the influence of the studied variables on the PMI values. The PMI value reflects how closely the printed interference colors align with the embossed pattern; thus, higher print quality corresponds to a higher PMI. The PMI of the printed BMFs decreased following a quadratic polynomial function with increasing BCNC and CCNC content in the ink. This reduction is attributed to the increased viscosity of the ink, which diminishes the effectiveness of surface tension-induced forces on the CNCs and polymer chains in the ink solution. Consequently, this negatively impacts the directed assembly of ink components along the DLBL, leading to a lower PMI value. Additionally, the spatial interlocking of CNC nanoparticles due to their rising concentration in the ink solution likely contributed to the observed deterioration in print quality. Consistent with the inherent behavior of CNCs in the liquid crystal phase, polarized light microscopy images revealed an increase in local interference color spots appearing outside the pattern area as CNC content in the ink rose (Chu et al., 2020; Fleming et al., 2001; Ghasemi et al., 2016, 2020; Gray & Mu, 2015; Kvien & Oksman, 2007; Mashkour et al., 2018; Prasher et al., 2006; Yue et al., 2022).

An evaluation of the effects of printing temperature on PMI values showed that increasing the printing temperature significantly decreased the PMI for both BCNC and CCNC inks. The impact of the higher viscosity of BCNC compared to CCNC becomes evident when assessing how printing temperature affects print quality. As the printing temperature rises, turbulence within the ink solution increases, and the printing time decreases, disrupting the directed assembly of CNCs along the DLBL (Araki et al., 1999; Mehmandoust et al., 2021; Mohsen-Nia & Modarress, 2006).

Furthermore, results indicated that reducing the γ-angle from 50° to 35° resulted in a decrease in the PMI of the prints. The PMI appears to be primarily influenced by factors affecting the intensity and effectiveness of the STT, including the γ-angle and the length and shape anisotropy of the CNCs. Notably, BCNC ink outperformed CCNC ink regarding PMI when the γ-angle was decreased from 50° to 35°. This advantage is attributed to the higher aspect ratio of BCNCs compared to CCNCs (Eichhorn, 2011; Kose et al., 2019; Pang et al., 2020; Prathapan et al., 2020; Yousefi et al., 2011), which likely mitigates the negative effects of the reduced γ-angle on STT magnitude. Visual representations of the variations in PMI affected by the examined variables are provided in Fig. 6 c–j, showcasing the differences between the printed BMFs.

The PSI index quantifies the quality of birefringent printing by comparing the symmetry of the interference colors in the printed BMFs to the symmetry of the embossed mold pattern. An increase in the PSI indicates improved print quality (Mehmandoust et al., 2021). The PSI was evaluated at a γ-angle of 50 degrees. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the trends in PSI changes for printed BMFs, as a function of CNC content in BCNC and CCNC inks, were nearly identical. Following a quadratic polynomial function, the PSI decreased with an increasing proportion of CNC in the ink formulation. This behavior, consistent with the observations regarding the PMI, is attributed to the impact of CNC weight percentage on the viscosity of the ink solution. Generally, prints made with CCNC ink exhibited a higher PSI than those made with BCNC ink under identical printing conditions and CNC content. As indicated by the viscosity evaluations, BCNC ink had a higher viscosity than CCNC ink at the same temperature and CNC concentration. The greater viscosity of the BCNC ink reduced the effectiveness of STT on the CNCs and polymer chains during printing, resulting in a lower PSI as an indicator of print quality (Chu et al., 2020; Heggset et al., 2017; Mehmandoust et al., 2021).

The effect of printing temperature on PSI changes exhibited differing behaviors depending on the ink used. For CCNC ink containing up to 6 w/w% CNC, the PSI increased with rising printing temperature. However, for CCNC inks with higher CNC content, increasing the printing temperature reduced the PSI. Conversely, a different trend was observed with BCNC inks. For BCNC inks with CNC content up to 6 w/w%, the PSI decreased as the printing temperature increased from 60 °C to 80 °C. In contrast, for BCNC inks with 8 and 10 w/w% CNC content, the PSI improved with rising printing temperature. This discrepancy can be attributed to the distinct geometric properties of CCNCs and BCNCs, as well as the effects of increased temperature on ink viscosity and molecular motion.

When using inks with CNC content below 8 w/w%, the decrease in viscosity with increasing temperature negatively impacted the PSI values. This reduction in viscosity coincided with undesirable increases in molecular motion and solvent phase evaporation rate, disrupting the directed assembly of CNCs and polymer chains along the DLBL (Mashkour et al., 2014, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021). Conversely, when the ink's CNC content exceeded 8 w/w%, increasing the printing temperature from 60°C to 80°C positively influenced PSI values. This improvement was due to reduced ink viscosity and enhanced performance of the STT driving forces. Fig. 7 b–g visually represent the variations in PSI of the printed BMFs influenced by the studied variable factors.

This study aimed to optimize the STT-based printing process by identifying and evaluating key variables affecting print quality, including CNC content and aspect ratio, printing temperature, and the metrological characteristics of the mold. Print quality was quantified using indices such as ICI, ICC, PMI, and PSI, confirming that parameter optimization significantly enhances the quality of BMFs produced by the STT-based printing process.

The findings revealed that increasing CNC concentration in both BCNC and CCNC inks notably raised ink viscosity and printing time. While ICI and ICC indices improved with higher CNC content, PMI and PSI indices showed a declining trend. BCNC ink exhibited enhanced viscosity at room temperature, leading to shorter printing times compared to CCNC ink, particularly at CNC concentrations of 4, 6, and 8 w/w%. Across all three-component inks and γ-angle values, both ICI and ICC indices consistently increased with rising CNC content. Optimal PSI and PMI performance was observed at 6 w/w% CNC concentration for both BCNC and CCNC inks. The ideal printing temperatures were determined to be 60 °C for BCNC ink and 80 °C for CCNC ink.

This research introduces a novel approach to birefringent microfigure printing by integrating optimized ink formulation with precise parameter control and quantitative evaluation metrics. These innovations enhance the precision and reproducibility of the printing process, offering scalable applications in advanced optical materials. The study confirms that parameter optimization significantly improves the quality of BMFs produced by the STT-based printing process. Under optimized conditions, nanocomposite films exhibit exceptional transparency, flexibility, and performance. These findings lay the groundwork for cellulose nanocrystal/polymer inks for multilayer anti-counterfeit security films and labels, addressing limitations of existing methods like extrusion and hydrogel printing. Additionally, the birefringent microfigures (BMFs) produced have potential applications in anti-counterfeiting systems, photonic devices, and high-precision sensors. The methodology also offers a path toward sustainable, cellulose-based materials, contributing to environmentally friendly solutions in optical and material science industries.

Mahboubeh Mehmandoust Kotlar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahdi Mashkour: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hossein Yousefi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Mehdi Tajvidi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the Vice Presidency of Research and Technology at Gorgon University of Agricultural Sciences and Natural Resources, and the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) under Grant No. 117014 for this research.
